gaps.title: Tenant isolation leak in ticket search
gaps.breaks: Support agents can see tickets from other tenants in search results
gaps.scenario: Agent at company A searches for "urgent" and sees tickets from company B that contain "urgent" in the subject line
gaps.prompt: Write a test that creates tickets in two different tenant companies, then simulates a support agent search and verifies that only tickets from their own company appear in results. Include edge cases where ticket subjects contain the search term but belong to different tenants.

gaps.title: Payment failure state not properly isolated
gaps.breaks: When a tenant's payment fails, their data might become accessible to other tenants during the transition
gaps.scenario: Company A's payment fails, but during the brief period before their access is revoked, an agent from Company B can accidentally access Company A's ticket data through a shared cache or connection pool
gaps.prompt: Write a test that simulates a payment failure scenario for one tenant while another tenant is actively using the system. Verify that the failing tenant's data is completely isolated and inaccessible throughout the entire payment failure process, including all cache layers and database connections.

gaps.title: Ticket count race condition with pricing tier changes
gaps.breaks: Simultaneous ticket creation and tier changes can cause incorrect billing counts
gaps.scenario: Company is at their 2,000th ticket limit, a customer creates a ticket while the system is simultaneously upgrading them to the Growth tier, resulting in the ticket being counted against both tiers or not counted at all
gaps.prompt: Write a test that creates multiple threads: one continuously creating tickets at the limit of a pricing tier, another thread simultaneously upgrading the tenant to the next tier, and a third thread checking the ticket count. Verify that all tickets are correctly counted against the appropriate tier and no tickets are lost or double-counted.

gaps.title: Webhook double-fire causing ticket duplication
gaps.breaks: Paddle might send the same payment webhook twice, creating duplicate tickets
gaps.scenario: Paddle sends a "payment_successful" webhook twice for the same transaction, the system processes both and creates two identical tickets for the same customer issue
gaps.prompt: Write a test that simulates receiving the same payment webhook payload twice in quick succession. Verify that the system detects and handles the duplicate webhook appropriately without creating duplicate tickets or causing other data inconsistencies.

gaps.title: Redis cache invalidation on tenant deletion
gaps.breaks: Cached data remains accessible after tenant account is deleted
gaps.scenario: Company A deletes their account, but cached ticket data remains in Redis and can be accessed by mistake if a new company with the same ID is created later
gaps.prompt: Write a test that creates a tenant, generates ticket data, verifies it's cached, then deletes the tenant. Confirm that all cached data for that tenant is completely removed from Redis. Then create a new tenant with the same ID and verify no stale data from the previous tenant is accessible.

raw_response:
GAPS FOUND: 5

GAP 1
Severity: CRITICAL
Title: Tenant isolation leak in ticket search
What breaks: Support agents can see tickets from other tenants in search results
Real scenario: Agent at company A searches for "urgent" and sees tickets from company B that contain "urgent" in the subject line
AI agent prompt: Write a test that creates tickets in two different tenant companies, then simulates a support agent search and verifies that only tickets from their own company appear in results. Include edge cases where ticket subjects contain the search term but belong to different tenants.

GAP 2
Severity: CRITICAL
Title: Payment failure state not properly isolated
What breaks: When a tenant's payment fails, their data might become accessible to other tenants during the transition
Real scenario: Company A's payment fails, but during the brief period before their access is revoked, an agent from Company B can accidentally access Company A's ticket data through a shared cache or connection pool
AI agent prompt: Write a test that simulates a payment failure scenario for one tenant while another tenant is actively using the system. Verify that the failing tenant's data is completely isolated and inaccessible throughout the entire payment failure process, including all cache layers and database connections.

GAP 3
Severity: HIGH
Title: Ticket count race condition with pricing tier changes
What breaks: Simultaneous ticket creation and tier changes can cause incorrect billing counts
Real scenario: Company is at their 2,000th ticket limit, a customer creates a ticket while the system is simultaneously upgrading them to the Growth tier, resulting in the ticket being counted against both tiers or not counted at all
AI agent prompt: Write a test that creates multiple threads: one continuously creating tickets at the limit of a pricing tier, another thread simultaneously upgrading the tenant to the next tier, and a third thread checking the ticket count. Verify that all tickets are correctly counted against the appropriate tier and no tickets are lost or double-counted.

GAP 4
Severity: HIGH
Title: Webhook double-fire causing ticket duplication
What breaks: Paddle might send the same payment webhook twice, creating duplicate tickets
Real scenario: Paddle sends a "payment_successful" webhook twice for the same transaction, the system processes both and creates two identical tickets for the same customer issue
AI agent prompt: Write a test that simulates receiving the same payment webhook payload twice in quick succession. Verify that the system detects and handles the duplicate webhook appropriately without creating duplicate tickets or causing other data inconsistencies.

GAP 5
Severity: MEDIUM
Title: Redis cache invalidation on tenant deletion
What breaks: Cached data remains accessible after tenant account is deleted
Real scenario: Company A deletes their account, but cached ticket data remains in Redis and can be accessed by mistake if a new company with the same ID is created later
AI agent prompt: Write a test that creates a tenant, generates ticket data, verifies it's cached, then deletes the tenant. Confirm that all cached data for that tenant is completely removed from Redis. Then create a new tenant with the same ID and verify no stale data from the previous tenant is accessible.
